statusLine: EU fuel security is under strain with 19 of 27 countries holding critical stock levels below mandatory minimums as oil prices surge past $99 per barrel.
fullAnalysis:
European fuel security faces significant pressure with widespread stock shortfalls across the bloc. A total of 19 of 27 EU countries are operating with critical fuel reserves below the mandatory 90-day minimum, while EU-wide averages hover precariously close to requirements at 98.7 days for petrol, 97.4 days for diesel, and 91.1 days for jet fuel.

Eastern European nations show the most severe deficits. Romania leads concerns with only 43.6 days of petrol reserves and a critically low 20.2 days of diesel stock. Poland follows with 51.8 days of petrol reserves and 21.3 days of jet fuel. Major economies are also affected, with Germany holding just 52.7 days of petrol reserves and France at 64.7 days, both well below the 90-day requirement.

The supply squeeze coincides with elevated oil prices, as Brent crude has reached $99.57 per barrel—up 4.89% recently and approximately 27% higher since the current Middle East conflict began. This price pressure is reflected in consumer costs, with EU average petrol at €1.784 per litre and diesel at €2.021 per litre, though significant price variations exist across member states.

While some countries maintain adequate reserves—notably Slovenia and Estonia with 365-day jet fuel stocks, and Cyprus with 192 days of petrol—the widespread nature of the shortfalls suggests systemic strain on European energy security infrastructure rather than isolated national issues.
keyPoints: 19 of 27 EU countries are operating with critical fuel reserves below the mandatory 90-day minimum
Eastern Europe faces the most severe shortages, with Romania holding only 20.2 days of diesel reserves
Major economies Germany and France have petrol stocks at 52.7 and 64.7 days respectively, well below requirements
Brent crude oil has surged to $99.57 per barrel, up 27% since the Middle East conflict began
EU-wide fuel averages remain barely above minimums at 98.7 days petrol and 97.4 days diesel
